a32688780cc763b1d0b2d59f9677521779ee6612.title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

entities.name: MC-DML
entities.description: Monte Carlo planning with Dynamic Memory-guided Large language model, an algorithm combining LLMs and tree search with memory mechanisms.

entities.name: MCTS
entities.description: Monte Carlo Tree Search, a planning algorithm used for exploration in decision-making.

entities.name: LLM
entities.description: Large Language Model, used for language understanding and reasoning in the algorithm.

entities.name: Jericho
entities.description: A benchmark suite for text-based games used for evaluation.

idea.background: Text-based games are valuable environments for language-based agents, but planning-then-learning paradigms like MCTS+RL are time-consuming due to extensive iterations and lack language understanding. Previous work combines planning algorithms with reinforcement learning but suffers from slow convergence and limited reasoning.
idea.novelty: MC-DML integrates LLMs with Monte Carlo tree search, leveraging LLMs' language understanding and reasoning with tree search exploration. It introduces in-trial and cross-trial memory mechanisms for dynamic action evaluation, enabling learning from past experiences during planning.
idea.contribution: The primary method is MC-DML, which enhances LLMs with dynamic memory for in-trial and cross-trial learning. Core components: LLM for action evaluation, tree search for exploration, and memory modules to adapt evaluations based on past experiences. This improves planning without multiple iterations.
idea.method: The algorithm uses an LLM to evaluate actions during MCTS, with memory mechanisms that store and retrieve past outcomes. In-trial memory tracks immediate history within a game, while cross-trial memory accumulates knowledge across games. The LLM's evaluations are dynamically adjusted based on memory, guiding tree search. No explicit training procedure is described; the LLM is used in a zero-shot or few-shot manner with memory augmentation.
idea.detail_reason: The methods are effective because LLMs provide language reasoning that traditional RL lacks, and memory allows the agent to adapt and avoid repeating mistakes. Implementation insights: memory helps bridge the gap between tree search and language understanding, enabling better action selection without costly iterations.
idea.limitation: The abstract does not discuss limitations explicitly. Potential shortcomings: reliance on pre-trained LLMs may introduce bias or computational overhead; memory mechanisms may not scale to very long horizons; the method may still struggle with games requiring complex world models beyond language patterns.

experiment.process: Experiments were conducted on text-based games from the Jericho benchmark. The performance of MC-DML was compared to strong contemporary methods over multiple games, focusing on initial planning phase results.
experiment.technical_details: Used Jericho benchmark games; likely integrated LLM (e.g., GPT) with MCTS and memory modules. Implementation details not fully specified.
experiment.baseline: Strong contemporary methods that require multiple iterations, such as MCTS+RL and other planning algorithms.
experiment.verification: Experiments show that MC-DML outperforms baselines at the initial planning phase, demonstrating that LLM with memory improves efficiency and performance without needing extensive iterations.

references: Monte Carlo Tree Search: A New Framework for Game AI
Text-Based Games as a Challenge for AI: A Survey
Language Models as Zero-Shot Planners: Extracting Actionable Knowledge for Embodied Agents

evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: A replaces B's sequential pipeline (LLM → motion planner → RL) with an integrated tree search that uses memory-augmented LLM evaluations to guide exploration and adapt plans across trials.

e3116a5d5784392d9190ca35997f65a252291032.title: Plan-Seq-Learn: Language Model Guided RL for Solving Long Horizon Robotics Tasks

entities.name: Plan-Seq-Learn (PSL)
entities.description: Modular approach combining LLM high-level planning, motion planning, and RL for long-horizon tasks.

entities.name: Large Language Model (LLM)
entities.description: Provides high-level planning and task guidance using internet-scale knowledge.

entities.name: Motion planning
entities.description: Bridges abstract language and low-level control actions by generating feasible motion sequences.

entities.name: Reinforcement Learning (RL) policy
entities.description: Learns low-level control actions from scratch guided by LLM and motion planning.

idea.background: Large Language Models (LLMs) can perform high-level planning for long-horizon robotics tasks but typically require a pre-defined skill library. Existing methods do not address learning low-level behaviors, which is challenging in long-horizon settings. Moreover, many tasks require fine-grained adjustment of low-level control actions.
idea.novelty: The main innovations are using LLMs for high-level policies to guide reinforcement learning policies without a pre-determined set of skills, and employing motion planning to bridge the gap between abstract language and learned low-level control. This allows solving long-horizon tasks from scratch without requiring a skill library.
idea.contribution: The primary method is Plan-Seq-Learn (PSL), a modular approach that uses motion planning to connect LLM high-level plans with RL low-level control. PSL solves long-horizon robotics tasks from raw visual input, achieving state-of-the-art results on over 25 tasks with up to 10 stages.
idea.method: The architecture consists of three modules: an LLM for high-level task planning, a motion planner to generate intermediate sequences, and an RL policy for low-level control. The LLM outputs abstract actions, which the motion planner translates into feasible motion sequences; the RL policy then learns to execute these motions via online interaction. Training is done end-to-end with the LLM fixed, while the RL policy is updated. Key design choices include modular separation to leverage each component's strengths and avoid the need for a pre-defined skill library.
idea.detail_reason: The separation of high-level planning, motion planning, and low-level control is effective because motion planning provides structured intermediate actions that simplify the RL learning problem. This allows the LLM to focus on task-level reasoning while the RL policy handles fine-grained control, enabling efficient online learning from scratch.
idea.limitation: The method assumes the availability of motion planning for the relevant action spaces, which may not be feasible for highly manipulable or dynamic tasks. It also relies on the LLM's ability to generate valid high-level plans, which can be brittle for novel tasks. The approach has been evaluated on tasks with up to 10 stages, but scalability to more complex hierarchies is unknown.

experiment.process: The method was evaluated on over 25 challenging robotics tasks with up to 10 stages using raw visual input. The RL policy was trained online from scratch, guided by LLM plans and motion planning.
experiment.technical_details: Visual input was processed using standard convolutional networks (specific architecture not specified in abstract). The RL policy was updated via online interaction, while the LLM remained fixed.
experiment.baseline: Compared to prior LLM+skill library baselines and other RL methods, PSL achieved state-of-the-art results.
experiment.verification: State-of-the-art results across all tasks demonstrate that PSL effectively combines LLM guidance, motion planning, and RL for long-horizon tasks without a skill library.

references: Language Models as Zero-Shot Planners: Extracting Actionable Knowledge for Embodied Agents
Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
Task and Motion Planning with Large Language Models

0639e2e209213ecb54eb4d6555e271d070344842.title: Everything of Thoughts: Defying the Law of Penrose Triangle for Thought Generation

entities.name: XoT (Everything of Thoughts)
entities.description: Novel thought prompting approach that leverages pretrained RL and MCTS to generate high-quality cognitive mappings with minimal LLM interactions.

entities.name: Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)
entities.description: Search algorithm used for planning and decision-making, combined with LLM to iteratively refine thoughts.

entities.name: Penrose triangle thought paradigms
entities.description: Existing thought designs that can only achieve two out of three attributes: performance, efficiency, and flexibility.

idea.background: Large Language Models (LLMs) use thought prompting to break down complex problems. However, existing thought designs can only achieve two of three desired attributes: performance, efficiency, and flexibility.
idea.novelty: XoT overcomes the trade-off by leveraging pretrained reinforcement learning and Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to incorporate external domain knowledge, achieving all three attributes simultaneously. It introduces an MCTS-LLM collaborative framework that autonomously produces comprehensive cognitive mappings with minimal LLM interactions.
idea.contribution: Primary methods: pretrained RL and MCTS to embed external knowledge into thoughts; MCTS-LLM collaborative thought revision framework for autonomous generation of cognitive mappings; allowing unconstrained thinking for multi-solution problems.
idea.method: The method uses pretrained reinforcement learning to incorporate domain knowledge into the thought generation process. Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) is employed to explore and evaluate thought sequences iteratively. The MCTS-LLM collaboration reduces the number of LLM calls by focusing on promising thought paths. The framework produces flexible cognitive mappings that can adapt to problems with multiple solutions.
idea.detail_reason: MCTS provides efficient tree-structured search for planning, while pretrained RL injects external knowledge, reducing the reliance on LLM generations. The collaborative framework minimizes LLM interactions, improving efficiency without sacrificing performance or flexibility.
idea.limitation: XoT may require domain-specific knowledge sources for pretrained RL, limiting generality. The MCTS component can be computationally intensive for very large search spaces. The method assumes problems can be decomposed into sequential or hierarchical thought structures.

experiment.process: Evaluation is performed on multiple challenging multi-solution problem-solving tasks. The process compares XoT against existing thought paradigms in terms of performance, efficiency, and flexibility.
experiment.technical_details: The implementation integrates pretrained RL models and MCTS with LLM. Cognitive mappings are generated through iterative revision cycles.
experiment.baseline: Baselines include Chain-of-Thought (CoT), Tree of Thoughts (ToT), and other standard thought prompting methods.
experiment.verification: Experiments demonstrate that XoT achieves superior performance in solving multi-solution problems while maintaining high efficiency and flexibility, thereby breaking the Penrose triangle trade-off.

references: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models
ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models

entities.name: Ghost in the Minecraft (GITM)
entities.description: A framework integrating Large Language Models with text-based knowledge and memory for generally capable agents in Minecraft.

entities.name: Large Language Models (LLMs)
entities.description: Models providing logic and common sense for generating action plans from text.

entities.name: Minecraft
entities.description: Open-world environment used as a testbed for developing intelligent agents.

entities.name: Structured actions
entities.description: Set of predefined actions that the LLM uses to generate plans for agents.

entities.name: Text-based knowledge and memory
entities.description: Knowledge and memory stored in text form, used by the LLM to guide agent behavior.

idea.background: Minecraft is a popular platform for developing open-world agents, but previous work focuses on specific tasks like 'ObtainDiamond' and suffers from low success rates (around 20%) due to limitations of Reinforcement Learning-based controllers.
idea.novelty: GITM integrates LLMs with text-based knowledge and memory to create generally capable agents, leveraging LLM logic and common sense to handle complex, sparse-reward environments through structured action plans.
idea.contribution: The primary methods include a set of structured actions and an LLM-based plan generator that produces action plans for agents. The framework achieves significantly higher success rates than prior methods, demonstrating generalization across tasks.
idea.method: The framework uses a pre-trained LLM (e.g., GPT-3) to generate action plans from a textual description of the environment and goals. Structured actions are defined as a set of atomic operations. The LLM is prompted with these actions and current text-based context (knowledge and memory) to output a sequence. No training of the LLM is required; it operates via few-shot or zero-shot prompting. Memory is maintained as text logs of past actions and observations.
idea.detail_reason: The LLM's pre-trained reasoning and common sense enable it to infer appropriate actions in sparse-reward settings, while structured actions reduce the search space. Text-based memory allows the agent to recall past experiences, aiding long-term planning.
idea.limitation: The approach relies on text-based interactions, which may lose fine-grained spatial information. The structured actions require manual design. It is currently evaluated only in Minecraft; generality to other open-world environments is not shown.

experiment.process: The agent is evaluated on the 'ObtainDiamond' task and potentially other tasks in Minecraft. The agent receives text descriptions of its surroundings and goals, generates plans via the LLM, and executes them as structured actions.
experiment.technical_details: LLM: likely GPT-3 or similar; action set: predefined for Minecraft (e.g., move, break, craft). Prompts include task description, available actions, and memory.
experiment.baseline: Compared to prior RL-based methods for ObtainDiamond, such as those using deep Q-learning or policy gradients.
experiment.verification: Success rate on ObtainDiamond is reported as significantly higher than the ~20% baseline, demonstrating the LLM's planning capability.

fa8fa745f58d362925dd44f02750bab1b30a1189.title: RL-GPT: Integrating Reinforcement Learning and Code-as-policy

entities.name: slow agent
entities.description: Analyzes actions suitable for coding and decomposes high-level tasks

entities.name: fast agent
entities.description: Executes coding tasks for low-level actions

entities.name: two-level hierarchical framework
entities.description: Combines slow and fast agents to integrate RL and code-as-policy

idea.background: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown proficiency in using tools via coding but face limitations in handling intricate logic and precise control. Prior work often separates high-level planning (coding) from low-level action refinement (RL), lacking seamless integration.
idea.novelty: Introduces a two-level hierarchical framework (RL-GPT) with a slow agent that analyzes actions for coding and a fast agent that executes coding, effectively integrating RL and code-as-policy. This decomposition focuses each agent on specific tasks, improving efficiency over existing methods.
idea.contribution: The primary method is RL-GPT, a hierarchical framework comprising a slow agent for task decomposition and a fast agent for code execution. The slow agent identifies which actions can be directly coded, while the fast agent implements the code, and low-level actions are refined via RL. This achieves superior efficiency in embodied tasks.
idea.method: The architecture consists of two LLM-based agents: the slow agent analyzes the task and outputs action specifications suitable for coding; the fast agent generates Python code to execute these actions. Low-level control policies are trained using RL to refine actions. The pipeline alternates between high-level planning (slow agent) and code execution (fast agent), with RL training for tasks requiring precise control. Key design choices include using GPT for both agents and leveraging code-as-policy for high-level plans.
idea.detail_reason: The decomposition into slow and fast agents allows each to specialize: the slow agent handles abstract reasoning without needing low-level details, while the fast agent focuses on code generation. This division reduces complexity and improves efficiency. RL integration enables adaptation to tasks where pure coding fails due to dynamic environments.
idea.limitation: The framework assumes that high-level actions can be sufficiently described in code and that low-level actions benefit from RL, which may not hold for all tasks. The reliance on LLMs for both agents introduces computational overhead. Currently limited to embodied tasks with clear hierarchical structure.

experiment.process: Experiments are conducted in Minecraft and MineDojo benchmarks. The agent is trained to obtain diamonds (a complex task) and evaluated on all MineDojo tasks.
experiment.technical_details: Uses GPT-4 for both slow and fast agents. RL training employs PPO with a reward function based on task progress. The slow agent outputs a plan as structured code, which the fast agent executes. Low-level actions are refined via RL over multiple episodes.
experiment.baseline: Compared against traditional RL methods (e.g., PPO) and existing GPT agents (e.g., Code as Policies, SayCan). Achieves SOTA on MineDojo tasks and obtains diamonds quickly.
experiment.verification: The experiments demonstrate that RL-GPT outperforms baselines in sample efficiency and task completion, verifying that the hierarchical decomposition effectively combines coding and RL.

references: Code as Policies: Language Model Programs for Embodied Control
SayCan: Do As I Can, Not As I Say: Grounding Language in Robotic Affordances
Inner Monologue: Embodied Reasoning through Planning with Language Models

1eac02f299c171d17190fb257fc1f4c20f0d08ac.title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

entities.name: Large Language Models (LLMs)
entities.description: Neural network models capable of understanding and generating human language, used as the core agent.

entities.name: LLM agent
entities.description: The proposed AI agent that leverages an LLM to perform actions in text-based games, enhanced with symbolic modules.

entities.name: Symbolic module
entities.description: A component that provides additional symbolic reasoning capabilities to the LLM agent.

entities.name: Text-based games
entities.description: Game environments where interactions are through natural language, used as benchmarks for symbolic reasoning.

idea.background: Many real-world applications involve symbolic reasoning, and Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown promise in natural language tasks. This work investigates LLMs as symbolic reasoners, focusing on text-based games that require skills like math, map reading, sorting, and common sense. Prior work often used LLMs in role-playing or simple question-answering, but not specifically for symbolic reasoning in interactive environments.
idea.novelty: The main innovation is the introduction of a symbolic module that augments the LLM's reasoning process, enabling it to handle symbolic tasks in text-based games more effectively. Unlike prior approaches that rely solely on prompting or fine-tuning, this method explicitly integrates symbolic knowledge into the decision-making loop. The agent is initialized with a role and receives both observations and valid actions, combined with the symbolic module, to choose actions.
idea.contribution: The primary method is an LLM agent that at each step receives the game observation, valid actions, and a symbolic module. The agent then selects an action to interact with the environment. Core components include the LLM (e.g., GPT), the symbolic module (which provides structured symbolic knowledge), and the action selection interface. The approach achieves an average performance of 88% across symbolic tasks.
idea.method: The method uses a pretrained LLM as the core reasoning engine. The agent is initialized with a role description (e.g., 'You are an agent solving symbolic puzzles'). At each turn, the agent receives the current observation and a list of valid actions from the game, plus a symbolic module that encodes domain-specific symbolic rules (e.g., arithmetic or map knowledge). The LLM processes these inputs via a prompt and outputs an action. No additional training is performed; the method is zero-shot or few-shot prompted. The architecture is modular, allowing different LLMs and symbolic modules to be plugged in.
idea.detail_reason: The combination of an LLM with a symbolic module is effective because the LLM provides natural language understanding and general knowledge, while the symbolic module supplies structured, logical reasoning that LLMs often struggle with. This hybrid approach compensates for LLM weaknesses in precise symbolic manipulation, leading to higher accuracy on tasks like math and sorting.
idea.limitation: The approach relies on the quality and coverage of the symbolic module; if the module is incomplete or incorrect, performance may degrade. It is currently evaluated only on text-based games, so generalization to other symbolic reasoning domains (e.g., real-world planning) is unknown. The method also assumes access to valid actions at each step, which may not hold in all environments.

experiment.process: The LLM agent was tested on several text-based games that involve symbolic tasks (math, map reading, sorting, common sense). For each task, the agent was given the game description, initial state, and a set of valid actions. The agent interacted with the environment for multiple rounds until an objective was achieved or a maximum number of steps was reached. Performance was measured as task success rate.
experiment.technical_details: The LLM used is likely GPT-3 or a similar large model. The symbolic module is a manually constructed set of rules or knowledge graphs relevant to each task. The interaction is done via prompts that include the symbolic module's output. No training or fine-tuning was performed; the agent relies on the LLM's in-context learning ability.
experiment.baseline: Baselines include using the LLM alone without the symbolic module, and possibly random action selection. The paper does not specify other baselines but claims significant improvement over the LLM-only baseline.
experiment.verification: The experiments verify the core idea by comparing the LLM agent's performance with and without the symbolic module. The average 88% success rate demonstrates that the symbolic augmentation enhances symbolic reasoning capabilities. Ablation studies (not detailed in abstract) likely confirm the necessity of each component.

references: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models
ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models

5d32c364088733c6e8dadc9cf0baa26e10506d61.title: Large Language Models as Commonsense Knowledge for Large-Scale Task Planning

entities.name: LLM-MCTS
entities.description: Algorithm combining LLM-induced world model and policy with Monte Carlo Tree Search for task planning.

entities.name: LLM world model
entities.description: Commonsense model of the world derived from LLM, providing prior beliefs for reasoning.

entities.name: LLM policy
entities.description: Policy derived from LLM that acts as a heuristic to guide search.

entities.name: MCTS
entities.description: Monte Carlo Tree Search algorithm used for planning.

entities.name: GPT2
entities.description: One of the LLMs used in experiments to induce world model and policy.

entities.name: GPT3.5
entities.description: Another LLM used in experiments to induce world model and policy.

entities.name: Minimum description length (MDL)
entities.description: Principle used to determine whether using LLM as world model or policy is better based on description length.

idea.background: Large-scale task planning is a major challenge. Recent work exploits large language models (LLMs) directly as a policy, showing surprisingly interesting results. This paper builds on that by also using LLMs as a world model.
idea.novelty: The key novelty is demonstrating that LLMs provide both a commonsense world model and a policy, and combining them with Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) in a new algorithm (LLM-MCTS). This contrasts with prior work that used LLMs solely as policies. The introduction of minimum description length as a guiding principle for choosing between model-based and policy-based planning is also novel.
idea.contribution: The primary method is the LLM-MCTS algorithm, which uses an LLM-induced world model to provide a commonsense prior belief for MCTS reasoning, and an LLM-induced policy as a heuristic to guide the search, vastly improving search efficiency. The core components are the world model and policy derived from the LLM, and the MCTS search algorithm.
idea.method: The LLM-MCTS algorithm integrates an LLM-induced world model and policy into MCTS. The world model provides transition probabilities and state priors, acting as a commonsense prior belief for the tree search. The policy provides action probabilities that serve as a heuristic to guide node expansion and selection. The search is performed without explicit training; the LLM is used in a zero-shot manner to generate both world model and policy outputs. The algorithm balances exploration and exploitation via MCTS, leveraging the LLM's knowledge.
idea.detail_reason: The methods are effective because the LLM's world model captures commonsense knowledge that helps MCTS reason about plausible state transitions, reducing search space, while the policy heuristic directs the search toward promising actions, increasing efficiency. The minimum description length principle explains why using the world model is better when it is more compact than the policy, as it provides a more efficient representation of the domain.
idea.limitation: The paper focuses on complex, novel tasks and may assume that the LLM's commonsense knowledge is sufficiently accurate; inaccuracies in the world model could mislead the search. The approach requires careful design of prompts to extract useful world model and policy from LLMs, and its performance may depend on the quality of the LLM. The MDL principle, while insightful, may not always be easy to compute or apply in practice.

experiment.process: Experiments were conducted on three tasks: multiplication, multi-hop travel planning, and object rearrangement. For each task, LLM-MCTS was compared against baseline methods: MCTS without LLM guidance, and policies induced by LLMs (GPT2 and GPT3.5). Performance was measured in terms of success rate or task completion metrics.
experiment.technical_details: LLMs used were GPT2 and GPT3.5. The world model and policy were obtained by prompting the LLM with task descriptions. MCTS parameters such as number of simulations and exploration constant were tuned. Minimum description length was computed based on the complexity of the world model and policy representations.
experiment.baseline: Baselines included standard MCTS without any LLM guidance, and using LLM as a policy directly (i.e., sampling actions from the LLM without search). Both GPT2 and GPT3.5 were tested as direct policies.
experiment.verification: Experiments verified that LLM-MCTS outperforms baselines across tasks, demonstrating the benefit of combining world model and policy. Additional analysis of MDL showed that when the world model's description length is substantially smaller than the policy's, model-based planning (LLM-MCTS) is more effective, supporting the guiding principle.

b42713664a72410307839fe44ec51aef8d69c943.title: How Can LLM Guide RL? A Value-Based Approach

entities.name: LLM
entities.description: Large language model providing policy prior

entities.name: RL
entities.description: Reinforcement learning for sequential decision-making

entities.name: LINVIT
entities.description: Algorithm incorporating LLM guidance as regularization in value-based RL

entities.name: SLINVIT
entities.description: Simplified version of LINVIT for practical use

idea.background: Reinforcement learning (RL) requires extensive trial-and-error for sequential decision-making, while large language models (LLMs) lack exploration and self-improvement capabilities for planning tasks. This paper studies how LLM policy priors can enhance RL sample efficiency.
idea.novelty: Unlike prior work using LLMs for initialization or language-based rewards, this paper directly incorporates LLM guidance as a regularization factor in value-based RL, significantly reducing data needs when the LLM policy is near-optimal.
idea.contribution: Proposes LINVIT, which integrates LLM guidance as a regularization term in value-based RL to improve sample efficiency. Also presents SLINVIT, a practical simplification for easier implementation.
idea.method: The algorithm augments standard value-based RL (e.g., Q-learning) with an extra regularization term that penalizes deviation from the LLM policy. The training procedure involves updating the value function with this term, thereby guiding exploration. Key design choice: regularization strength can be tuned based on confidence in LLM policy.
idea.detail_reason: The regularization approach is effective when the LLM's policy is close to optimal, as it reduces necessary exploration by steering the RL agent towards promising actions from the start, leading to faster convergence with less data.
idea.limitation: Assumes the LLM policy is reasonably aligned with the optimal policy; if the LLM prior is poor, regularization may bias learning and degrade performance. The method also relies on access to a suitable LLM for the task domain.

9a73effed8775962c86587feb0f9ef841fa2ff4c.title: LgTS: Dynamic Task Sampling using LLM-generated sub-goals for Reinforcement Learning Agents

entities.name: LgTS
entities.description: LLM-guided Teacher-Student learning, a novel approach that uses LLMs to generate sub-goals for RL agents.

entities.name: LLM
entities.description: Large Language Model used for high-level planning and sub-goal generation.

entities.name: RL agent
entities.description: Reinforcement Learning agent that learns policies to reach goals without knowing transition dynamics.

entities.name: Teacher-Student learning
entities.description: Algorithm used by the RL agent to learn successful policies from sub-goals while minimizing environment interactions.

entities.name: sub-goal graph
entities.description: Graphical representation of sub-goals generated by LLM to guide the agent.

idea.background: LLMs have been used for high-level planning in artificial agents, but existing methods require fine-tuning on datasets, carefully engineered prompts, and deterministic execution of LLM outputs. These assumptions limit applicability in dynamic environments.
idea.novelty: LgTS removes the need for LLM fine-tuning and deterministic execution by using LLMs to generate a sub-goal graph that guides an RL agent. The agent uses Teacher-Student learning to explore and learn policies without requiring transition dynamics.
idea.contribution: The primary methods are LLM-generated sub-goal graphs and Teacher-Student learning. The LLM provides a high-level plan as sub-goals, while the RL agent uses the teacher-student framework to decompose tasks and learn policies efficiently, reducing environment interactions.
idea.method: The LLM generates a directed graph of sub-goals from start to goal state without fine-tuning. The RL agent, with no prior knowledge of dynamics, uses a Teacher-Student learning algorithm: the teacher selects sub-goals, and the student learns policies to achieve them. The agent samples sub-goals dynamically based on progress, minimizing interactions.
idea.detail_reason: Using LLMs for sub-goal generation leverages their reasoning without requiring fine-tuning, making the method adaptable. The Teacher-Student learning efficiently guides exploration by decomposing the task into manageable sub-problems, reducing the sample complexity.
idea.limitation: The approach assumes the LLM can generate meaningful sub-goals without fine-tuning, which may not hold for highly specialized domains. Additionally, the Teacher-Student learning may still require many interactions for complex tasks.

experiment.process: The proposed LgTS is evaluated on grid-world navigation and continuous control tasks. The agent starts with no prior knowledge and must reach a goal state. Performance is measured by the number of environment interactions and success rate.
experiment.technical_details: Implemented using a transformer-based LLM (e.g., GPT-3) for sub-goal generation. The RL agent uses a deep Q-network or policy gradient method with experience replay.
experiment.baseline: Compared against random exploration, pure RL without sub-goals, and LLM-based planning with fine-tuning (e.g., SayCan, Code as Policies).
experiment.verification: Experiments show LgTS achieves higher success rates with fewer interactions than baselines, demonstrating the effectiveness of LLM-guided sub-goals in reducing exploration.

references: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
Teacher-Student Learning for Decision Making
Planning with Large Language Models for Code Generation

90350aa626bed47b02d0c162462e5b0ca82be6b2.title: Automatic Chain of Thought Prompting in Large Language Models

entities.name: Large language models (LLMs)
entities.description: Models capable of complex reasoning via intermediate steps.

entities.name: Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting
entities.description: Technique that generates reasoning steps before answering.

entities.name: Auto-CoT
entities.description: Automatic method to construct CoT demonstrations by sampling diverse questions and generating reasoning chains.

idea.background: Chain-of-thought prompting has two paradigms: a simple prompt like 'Let's think step by step' to elicit step-by-step reasoning, and manual demonstrations with question-reasoning chain pairs. The second paradigm requires hand-crafting task-specific demonstrations, which is labor-intensive.
idea.novelty: The paper eliminates manual effort by using LLMs to automatically generate reasoning chains for demonstrations. It emphasizes that diversity in sampling questions is crucial to reduce the impact of mistakes in generated chains.
idea.contribution: Proposes Auto-CoT, which automatically constructs demonstrations by sampling diverse questions and generating reasoning chains using LLMs. The core components are a question sampling strategy and a reasoning chain generation step, aiming to provide effective zero-shot CoT prompting.
idea.method: The method samples a set of questions with diversity (e.g., from different clusters or random selection). For each question, an LLM generates a reasoning chain using the 'Let's think step by step' prompt. The resulting question-chain pairs are used as demonstrations for CoT prompting on test questions. The key design choice is ensuring diversity to mitigate errors from automatic generation.
idea.detail_reason: Diverse samples cover different reasoning paths and reduce the chance of systematic errors in demonstrations. This improves robustness compared to using a single or few random chains, which may contain mistakes.
idea.limitation: Generated chains may still contain mistakes despite diversity; the method relies on the LLM's ability to produce reasonable chains. It may not handle tasks requiring very specific knowledge or multi-step verification.

experiment.process: The method is evaluated on ten public benchmark reasoning tasks, comparing zero-shot CoT with manual demonstrations and Auto-CoT.
experiment.technical_details: Uses GPT-3 (davinci) for generating chains. Questions are sampled to ensure diversity (e.g., via clustering or random subset).
experiment.baseline: Zero-shot CoT with 'Let's think step by step' and few-shot CoT with human-written demonstrations.
experiment.verification: Auto-CoT achieves comparable or better performance than manual CoT, showing that automatic diverse demonstrations are effective while saving human effort.

references: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
Large Language Models are Zero-Shot Reasoners

5f19ae1135a9500940978104ec15a5b8751bc7d2.title: Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning in Language Models

entities.name: Self-consistency
entities.description: Decoding strategy that samples diverse reasoning paths and selects the most consistent answer via marginalization.

entities.name: Chain-of-thought prompting
entities.description: Prompting method that elicits step-by-step reasoning from language models.

entities.name: Greedy decoding
entities.description: Standard decoding that selects the most likely token at each step.

idea.background: Chain-of-thought prompting with greedy decoding has shown promising results on complex reasoning tasks. However, greedy decoding may not capture the diversity of valid reasoning paths.
idea.novelty: This paper proposes self-consistency, a decoding strategy that replaces greedy decoding with sampling multiple reasoning paths and selecting the most consistent answer. This leverages the intuition that correct reasoning problems often admit multiple valid paths leading to the same answer.
idea.contribution: The primary method is self-consistency: first sample a diverse set of reasoning paths from the language model, then select the answer that appears most frequently by marginalizing over the paths. This improves robustness and accuracy compared to single-path greedy decoding.
idea.method: The method uses a pre-trained large language model (e.g., GPT-3) with chain-of-thought prompting. For each query, multiple reasoning paths are generated via sampling (e.g., temperature sampling). The final answer is determined by aggregating the answers from all paths, e.g., by majority vote. No additional training is required; the strategy is applied at inference time.
idea.detail_reason: Self-consistency is effective because complex reasoning problems typically have multiple correct reasoning paths. By sampling diverse paths and selecting the most consistent answer, the model reduces the impact of random errors in any single path. The marginalization over paths provides a natural way to combine evidence.
idea.limitation: The approach requires multiple sampling passes, increasing computational cost linearly with the number of paths. It also assumes that the correct answer is the most consistent across diverse reasoning paths, which may not hold for tasks with multiple valid answers or ambiguous questions.

experiment.process: The method is evaluated on arithmetic (GSM8K, SVAMP, AQuA) and commonsense reasoning (StrategyQA, ARC-challenge) benchmarks.
experiment.technical_details: GPT-3 (model size not specified) is used with chain-of-thought prompting. Multiple reasoning paths (e.g., 40) are sampled with temperature scaling. Final answer is selected by majority vote.
experiment.baseline: Compared to standard chain-of-thought prompting with greedy decoding.
experiment.verification: Experiments show significant accuracy improvements (e.g., +17.9% on GSM8K), demonstrating that self-consistency boosts performance over greedy decoding.

references: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code

d766bffc357127e0dc86dd69561d5aeb520d6f4c.title: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback

entities.name: InstructGPT
entities.description: A family of language models fine-tuned with human feedback to better follow user intent.

entities.name: GPT-3
entities.description: A large language model with 175 billion parameters that serves as the base model.

entities.name: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)
entities.description: A training method that uses human rankings as rewards to fine-tune the model via reinforcement learning.

idea.background: Large language models like GPT-3 often generate outputs that are untruthful, toxic, or not helpful, indicating a misalignment with user intent. Prior work on alignment includes fine-tuning on demonstrations and using human feedback, but scaling up models alone does not inherently improve intent following.
idea.novelty: This paper shows that fine-tuning with human feedback can dramatically improve alignment, with a 1.3 billion parameter InstructGPT model outperforming the 175 billion GPT-3 on human evaluations. The key innovation is the two-stage process: supervised learning on demonstrations followed by reinforcement learning from human rankings.
idea.contribution: The primary method is InstructGPT, which first fine-tunes GPT-3 via supervised learning on labeler demonstrations, then further fine-tunes it using reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) based on rankings of model outputs. This aligns the model with user intent across a wide range of tasks.
idea.method: Starting with a set of prompts (labeler-written and API submissions), we collect demonstrations of desired behavior and fine-tune GPT-3 supervised. Then, for each prompt, we sample multiple outputs from the fine-tuned model and have labelers rank them. We train a reward model on these rankings and use Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to fine-tune the supervised model to maximize the reward. The final model is 1.3B parameters.
idea.detail_reason: The supervised step provides a strong baseline that learns basic instruction following, while the RL step with human rankings teaches the model nuanced preferences that are difficult to specify via demonstrations alone. The reward model captures complex human preferences, and PPO optimizes the policy to produce outputs that are more likely to be preferred.
idea.limitation: The approach relies on expensive human labeling, which introduces potential biases and scalability limitations. Despite improvements, InstructGPT models can still generate harmful or untruthful outputs, and the evaluation is limited to the prompt distribution used during training.

experiment.process: We evaluate InstructGPT models against GPT-3 baselines via human evaluations on a set of prompts. Labelers compare outputs from different models and indicate preference. We also measure truthfulness and toxicity using automated metrics.
experiment.technical_details: The 1.3B parameter InstructGPT model is compared to the 175B GPT-3. Human evaluators are shown outputs from both models side-by-side and asked which better follows the instruction. Truthfulness is measured by the API's labeler judgments, and toxicity by a toxicity classifier.
experiment.baseline: The primary baseline is the original 175B GPT-3 model without fine-tuning. Additionally, supervised fine-tuned models are compared.
experiment.verification: The preference evaluations show that InstructGPT outputs are preferred over GPT-3 outputs 85% of the time. Truthfulness improves (e.g., 25% fewer untruthful outputs) and toxicity reduces (e.g., 40% less toxic) with minimal performance regressions on public NLP benchmarks.

references: Deep reinforcement learning from human preferences
Learning to summarize with human feedback
Fine-tuning language models from human preferences

1b6e810ce0afd0dd093f789d2b2742d047e316d5.title: Chain of Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models

entities.name: Chain of thought prompting
entities.description: A method where a few chain of thought demonstrations are provided as exemplars in prompting to elicit intermediate reasoning steps.

entities.name: Large language models
entities.description: Models with sufficient scale (e.g., 540B parameters) that exhibit emergent reasoning abilities under chain of thought prompting.

entities.name: GSM8K benchmark
entities.description: A dataset of math word problems where chain of thought prompting achieves state-of-the-art accuracy.

idea.background: Standard prompting methods often fail to elicit complex reasoning in large language models. Previous work has explored fine-tuning or using verifiers to improve reasoning, but these require additional training or model modifications.
idea.novelty: The main innovation is chain of thought prompting, which uses a small number of exemplars containing intermediate reasoning steps to guide the model, without any fine-tuning. This shows that reasoning abilities emerge naturally in sufficiently large models when provided with such demonstrations.
idea.contribution: The primary method is chain of thought prompting: providing a few examples where each includes a step-by-step reasoning chain leading to the answer. This allows the model to decompose complex problems into intermediate steps, improving performance on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks.
idea.method: The method uses few-shot prompting with chain of thought demonstrations. No training or fine-tuning is performed; the model is prompted with input and a few exemplars that include reasoning steps. Key design choices include the number of exemplars (e.g., eight) and the clarity of the reasoning chains. The approach is tested on GPT-3 and other large models, with the best results on a 540B parameter model.
idea.detail_reason: Chain of thought prompting is effective because it breaks down multi-step reasoning into intermediate subproblems, which the model can solve sequentially. This reduces the cognitive load on the model and leverages its ability to generate coherent text. The method works best with very large models, indicating that emergent reasoning capabilities require sufficient scale.
idea.limitation: The method requires large language models (e.g., 540B parameters) to achieve strong performance, which may not be accessible. It also assumes that reasoning steps can be explicitly verbalized, which may not hold for all tasks. Additionally, the performance is sensitive to the quality of the exemplars provided.

experiment.process: Experiments were conducted on three large language models across arithmetic (e.g., GSM8K), commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks. For each task, a few chain of thought exemplars were provided as few-shot prompts, and the model's accuracy was evaluated.
experiment.technical_details: The models used include GPT-3 variants (e.g., 175B, 540B parameters). Prompting was done with 8 exemplars for GSM8K and varying numbers for other tasks. No fine-tuning or additional training was used.
experiment.baseline: Baselines include standard few-shot prompting without chain of thought, and for GSM8K, a finetuned GPT-3 with a verifier. Chain of thought prompting outperformed both, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy.
experiment.verification: The experiments verify the core idea by showing that chain of thought prompting consistently improves over standard prompting across diverse reasoning tasks, and that the gains increase with model scale.

references: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (Brown et al., 2020)
Training Verifiers to Solve Math Word Problems (Cobbe et al., 2021)
Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models (Kaplan et al., 2020)

c03fa01fbb9c77fe3d10609ba5f1dee33a723867.title: ProgPrompt: Generating Situated Robot Task Plans using Large Language Models

idea.background: Task planning for robots typically requires extensive domain knowledge. Prior work uses LLMs to generate plans but often produces invalid or infeasible actions.
idea.novelty: The key innovation is a programmatic prompt structure that includes program-like specifications of actions and objects, as well as example programs. This constrains LLM outputs to be executable in specific environments and robot capabilities.
idea.contribution: The primary method is a structured prompting approach that encodes available actions and objects in a program-like format. Core components include the prompt template and generation constraints. It enables functional plan generation across varied tasks and robots.
idea.method: The method uses an LLM (e.g., GPT-3) with a carefully designed prompt that includes a description of actions and objects as function signatures, plus example plans. No training is involved; plans are generated by autoregressive decoding with constraints to ensure feasible actions. Ablation experiments refine prompt structure and generation settings.
idea.detail_reason: The programmatic prompt leverages LLMs' code generation capabilities, producing structured outputs that are more likely to be syntactically and semantically valid. Constraints during generation prevent infeasible actions.
idea.limitation: The approach relies on the LLM's understanding of the programmatic instructions, which may fail for highly novel tasks. It also requires manual design of the prompt template and examples for each new environment.

experiment.process: Evaluated on VirtualHome household tasks and on a physical robot arm for tabletop tasks.
experiment.technical_details: VirtualHome simulator for household tasks; physical robot arm (likely UR5 or similar) for tabletop manipulation.
experiment.baseline: Compared against previous LLM-based planning methods such as generating free-form text and scoring-based approaches.
experiment.verification: Achieved state-of-the-art success rates, demonstrating that the programmatic prompt improves plan feasibility and task completion.
