91deaf9d324c8feafc189da0da03e60a60287bca.title: Code as Policies: Language Model Programs for Embodied Control

entities.name: Code as Policies
entities.description: A formulation that uses language model generated programs (LMPs) for robot control.

entities.name: Language Model Programs (LMPs)
entities.description: Programs generated by LLMs to implement robot policies from natural language commands.

entities.name: Large Language Models (LLMs)
entities.description: Code-completion trained models used to synthesize robot policy code.

entities.name: Object detectors
entities.description: Perception modules that provide input to policy code, e.g., [2], [3].

entities.name: Control primitive APIs
entities.description: Low-level robot control functions that are parameterized by generated policy code.

entities.name: Third-party libraries (NumPy, Shapely)
entities.description: Libraries used for arithmetic and spatial-geometric reasoning in policy code.

idea.background: Large language models trained on code-completion have shown the ability to synthesize simple Python programs from docstrings. This work builds on that capability, repurposing such models to generate robot policy code from natural language commands, enabling embodied control without task-specific training.
idea.novelty: The key innovation is re-purposing code-writing LLMs to directly produce executable robot policy code via few-shot prompting, rather than using LLMs for high-level planning or text generation. This approach enables spatial-geometric reasoning through library calls, generalization to new instructions, and contextual interpretation of vague terms (e.g., 'faster').
idea.contribution: The primary method is few-shot prompting: providing several example language commands (as comments) and corresponding policy code as input, then asking the LLM to generate new code for a given command. Core components include chaining classic logic structures (if-else, loops) and calling third-party libraries (NumPy, Shapely) to perform arithmetic and spatial reasoning. The generated code outputs parameterized API calls for control primitives.
idea.method: Input consists of few-shot examples formatted as comments (language commands) followed by Python policy code. The LLM (trained on code completion) is prompted to generate new code for a new command. The generated code uses classical control flow and invokes perception APIs (e.g., object detector outputs) and control APIs. It also imports libraries like NumPy and Shapely for numerical and geometric operations. No additional training or fine-tuning is performed; the model's existing code synthesis ability is exploited.
idea.detail_reason: LLMs trained on code are effective because they learn to compose functions and handle diverse API calls without further training. Using comments as prompts leverages the natural mapping from docstrings to code, making the conversion from natural language to policy straightforward. The reliance on external libraries allows the model to offload complex arithmetic and geometry, overcoming limitations of pure text generation.
idea.limitation: The approach depends heavily on the quality and diversity of few-shot examples—poor examples lead to incorrect code. It also requires that all needed perception and control APIs are pre-defined; complex tasks may need many such APIs. The model may generate syntactically valid but semantically incorrect code, especially for novel scenarios not covered by examples.

experiment.process: Experiments likely involve presenting the LLM with few-shot examples of language commands and corresponding policy code, then asking it to generate code for new commands in a tabletop manipulation setting. The generated code is executed on a robot to perform tasks like moving objects or adjusting velocities, and success rates are measured.
experiment.technical_details: Use of a pre-trained LLM (e.g., Codex) with few-shot prompting. Perception via object detectors (e.g., from [2], [3]). Control via API calls for robot arm. Libraries: NumPy for calculations, Shapely for geometric operations.
experiment.baseline: Compare to prior methods that may use human-written policies or learn from demonstration. Likely no baseline that uses LLMs for direct code generation in this context.
experiment.verification: Experiments verify that the generated code exhibits spatial reasoning, generalizes to new instructions, and correctly interprets ambiguous descriptions ('faster') in context, as demonstrated by successful task completion.

references: Program Synthesis with Large Language Models
Learning to Manipulate Object Detectors for Robotics
Robot Control via Code-Generating Language Models

32c9b3859086d15184989454eb878638659e64c6.title: MineDojo: Building Open-Ended Embodied Agents with Internet-Scale Knowledge

entities.name: MineDojo
entities.description: A framework built on Minecraft for building open-ended embodied agents with internet-scale knowledge.

entities.name: Minecraft
entities.description: A popular game used as the simulation environment for diverse tasks and knowledge acquisition.

entities.name: Simulation suite
entities.description: Part of MineDojo featuring thousands of diverse open-ended tasks.

entities.name: Knowledge base
entities.description: Internet-scale database comprising Minecraft videos, tutorials, wiki pages, and forum discussions.

entities.name: Agent learning algorithm
entities.description: Novel algorithm leveraging pre-trained video-language models as a learned reward function.

entities.name: Video-language model
entities.description: Large pre-trained model used to provide reward signals for agent training without manual shaping.

idea.background: Autonomous agents have achieved success in specialist domains like Atari and Go but typically learn tabula rasa in isolated environments with manually conceived objectives, failing to generalize across diverse tasks. Prior work lacks a trinity of open-ended environment, large-scale multimodal knowledge, and flexible agent architecture.
idea.novelty: The main innovation is the introduction of the trinity of ingredients for generalist agents: an environment supporting many tasks, an internet-scale multimodal knowledge base, and a flexible agent architecture. Additionally, a novel learning algorithm uses pre-trained video-language models as a reward function, eliminating manually designed dense shaping rewards.
idea.contribution: The paper presents MineDojo, a framework with a Minecraft simulation suite of thousands of tasks and an internet-scale knowledge base. It proposes an agent learning algorithm that leverages large pre-trained video-language models to provide learned rewards, enabling agents to solve open-ended tasks specified in free-form language without manual reward engineering.
idea.method: The agent architecture uses a pre-trained video-language model (e.g., based on CLIP or VideoBERT) to encode video demonstrations and language instructions. The reward function is computed as similarity between the agent's current observation and goal description in the joint embedding space. Training employs reinforcement learning with this learned reward, without manually designed dense rewards. The knowledge base provides diverse training data for the video-language model.
idea.detail_reason: The method is effective because it aligns the agent's learning objective with human-like understanding of goals from video and language, leveraging large-scale pre-training to generalize across tasks. Implementation insights include using contrastive learning to align video and text embeddings, and mining diverse tasks from the knowledge base to avoid overfitting.
idea.limitation: Current limitations include reliance on the Minecraft domain, which may not transfer to other environments. The agent may struggle with tasks requiring precise control or long-term planning not well captured by video-language rewards. Assumptions include availability of large-scale video-text data for pre-training.

experiment.process: The agent is trained on a set of open-ended tasks specified in free-form language, using the learned reward from the video-language model. Training involves interaction with the MineDojo simulation suite, with performance measured by task success rate and goal completion.
experiment.technical_details: The video-language model is pre-trained on the MineDojo knowledge base using contrastive learning. The agent uses a policy network (e.g., PPO) with visual and language inputs. Reward is computed as cosine similarity between encoded observation and goal text.
experiment.baseline: Baselines include task-specific reward functions and other RL agents without the learned reward (e.g., behavior cloning from videos, sparse reward RL). Comparisons show the learned reward significantly improves sample efficiency and final performance.
experiment.verification: Experiments verify the core idea by demonstrating that the agent can solve novel tasks not seen during training, and that the learned reward correlates well with human judgment. Ablation studies confirm the importance of the internet-scale knowledge base and the video-language model.

references: Playing Atari with Deep Reinforcement Learning
Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision (CLIP)
Minecraft as a Platform for General-Purpose AI Research

6b7bf6cf5d0e1004d4468edfac4e8f81c0d0c50d.title: MineRL Diamond 2021 Competition: Overview, Results, and Lessons Learned

entities.name: MineRL Diamond 2021 Competition
entities.description: Third edition of the MineRL ObtainDiamond competition with a two-track design.

entities.name: MineRL ObtainDiamond task
entities.description: Reinforcement learning task to obtain a diamond in the MineRL environment.

entities.name: Two-track design
entities.description: Easier track allowing any solution and harder track enforcing generalizable methods.

idea.background: Reinforcement learning competitions advance the field by providing scope and support for specific problems. Organizers enforce general techniques, sample efficiency, and reproducibility, but these restrictions increase difficulty. The MineRL Diamond 2021 competition addressed this by introducing a separate easier track for newcomers.
idea.novelty: The main innovation is the two-track system: an easier track allowing any solution to encourage participation, and a harder track enforcing generalizable solutions. This lowered the barrier for entry while still advancing generalizable methods.
idea.contribution: The competition provided extensive tutorials and support. The easier track successfully obtained a diamond, and the harder track progressed generalizable solutions.
idea.method: The competition used the MineRL environment with the ObtainDiamond task. The harder track required sample-efficient and generalizable RL algorithms. Exact technical details are not provided in the abstract.
idea.detail_reason: The two-track design was effective because it allowed newcomers to participate without stringent restrictions, increasing submissions, while the harder track maintained focus on generalizable approaches.
idea.limitation: The harder track still had a high barrier, possibly limiting participation. The competition may not fully address the trade-off between generalizability and performance.

experiment.process: The competition was held with two tracks. Participants submitted solutions. The easier track allowed any method; the harder track required general techniques.
experiment.technical_details: The MineRL environment and ObtainDiamond task were used. Specific algorithms are not detailed in the abstract.
experiment.baseline: No explicit baselines mentioned; comparison between the two tracks.
experiment.verification: Results showed increased submissions and successful diamond acquisition in the easier track, while the harder track progressed generalizable solutions.

65fc1f1c567801fee3788974e753cdbf934f07e9.title: Video PreTraining (VPT): Learning to Act by Watching Unlabeled Online Videos

entities.name: Video PreTraining (VPT)
entities.description: Semi-supervised imitation learning method that pretrains agents by watching unlabeled online videos, using an inverse dynamics model to label them.

entities.name: Inverse Dynamics Model (IDM)
entities.description: Model trained on a small amount of labeled data to predict actions given two consecutive frames, used to label unlabeled video data.

entities.name: Behavioral Prior
entities.description: General policy trained via behavior cloning on the labeled dataset (original + IDM-labeled), capable of zero-shot performance and fine-tuning.

idea.background: Sequential decision domains like robotics and games lack large labeled datasets for pretraining behavioral priors. Previous work pretrains on text/images but not on action-labeled data. VPT addresses this by leveraging unlabeled online videos.
idea.novelty: Extends internet-scale pretraining to sequential decision domains using semi-supervised imitation learning. Key innovation is training an inverse dynamics model with a small labeled dataset to label a huge unlabeled video corpus, enabling behavioral prior learning from unlabeled data.
idea.contribution: Primary method: semi-supervised imitation learning with inverse dynamics labeling. Core components: inverse dynamics model (predicts actions from frames), behavioral prior (trained via behavior cloning on labeled data). Functions: label unlabeled videos, then distill a policy with zero-shot and fine-tuning capabilities.
idea.method: Architecture: inverse dynamics model takes two consecutive frames (mouse/keyboard at 20Hz) and outputs action probabilities. Training: first train IDM on small labeled Minecraft dataset (e.g., contractor data). Then use IDM to label large unlabeled YouTube Minecraft videos. Finally, train behavioral prior via behavior cloning on combined labeled data. Fine-tuning uses reinforcement learning (e.g., PPO) or further imitation learning on specific tasks.
idea.detail_reason: Using human interface (mouse/keyboard at 20Hz) enables direct mapping from videos to actions. IDM is accurate enough with minimal labeled data. Behavioral prior captures broad gameplay, providing zero-shot exploration, and fine-tuning solves hard-exploration tasks that RL from scratch fails on.
idea.limitation: Requires a small amount of labeled data for training IDM, and assumes action space is consistent between labeled and unlabeled data. Evaluated only in Minecraft; generalization to other domains not shown. Performance may degrade with large domain shift.

experiment.process: Train IDM on 2,000 hours of labeled Minecraft play; label 70,000 hours of unlabeled YouTube Minecraft videos; train behavioral prior via behavior cloning; fine-tune on hard-exploration tasks (e.g., obtain diamond) using RL and imitation learning.
experiment.technical_details: Inverse dynamics model: ResNet-based CNN processing frame pairs; behavior cloning: supervised learning over actions; RL fine-tuning: PPO with reward shaping from task completions.
experiment.baseline: RL from scratch (PPO without pretraining), behavior cloning from limited labeled data, and random policy.
experiment.verification: Zero-shot evaluation: behavioral prior obtains items and performs tasks without fine-tuning. Fine-tuned VPT significantly outperforms RL-from-scratch on tasks requiring long-horizon exploration, demonstrating that pretraining provides useful prior knowledge.

references: Learning to Act from Unlabeled Videos via Inverse Dynamics
Imitation Learning from Observation with Inverse Dynamics
Minecraft as a Testbed for Deep Reinforcement Learning

f3cf71c51b882fe3111d71c4bf104297d38197f8.title: Inner Monologue: Embodied Reasoning through Planning with Language Models

entities.name: Inner Monologue
entities.description: A reasoning mechanism that uses environment feedback to form an internal dialogue for planning.

entities.name: Large Language Models (LLMs)
entities.description: Pre-trained language models used for planning and reasoning in embodied environments.

entities.name: Environment Feedback
entities.description: Natural language feedback from the environment, including success detection, scene description, and human interaction.

entities.name: Robotic Control
entities.description: The domain of applying LLMs to control robots through high-level instructions.

idea.background: Recent works have applied LLMs to embodied tasks like robot planning and interaction, requiring understanding of skills, their effects, and linguistic mapping. Prior approaches often need fine-tuning or predefined skill sets, limiting adaptability.
idea.novelty: The key innovation is using LLMs in closed-loop with natural language feedback from the environment without additional training. This enables an 'inner monologue' that processes real-time feedback to refine plans, improving instruction completion over open-loop methods.
idea.contribution: The primary method is to augment LLM-based planning with environmental feedback (success detection, scene descriptions, human input) via a closed-loop system. Core components include feedback parsing and plan revision, all without fine-tuning the LLM.
idea.method: The LLM receives a high-level instruction and generates a sequence of skills. At each step, the environment provides natural language feedback, which is fed back into the LLM as part of the prompt to simulate inner monologue. No additional training is performed; the LLM is used off-the-shelf with zero-shot prompting. Feedback types include binary success, descriptive scene text, and human commands.
idea.detail_reason: Using natural language feedback allows the LLM to leverage its pre-trained reasoning capabilities over text, avoiding the need for task-specific training or reward engineering. The inner monologue structure enables the model to consider contextual changes and adjust plans dynamically.
idea.limitation: The approach assumes reliable and informative feedback from the environment, which may not always be available or accurate. It may struggle with tasks requiring precise low-level control or long-horizon planning where feedback is sparse or ambiguous.

experiment.process: Experiments were conducted on three robotic domains (e.g., tabletop manipulation, navigation) measuring high-level instruction completion rate. The agent iteratively plans and receives feedback after each action.
experiment.technical_details: LLM used is GPT-3 or similar. Feedback is preprocessed into natural language. No fine-tuning; prompts are engineered to incorporate feedback history.
experiment.baseline: Compared against open-loop planning without feedback, and ablations removing specific feedback types.
experiment.verification: Results show that closed-loop feedback significantly improves task completion across all three domains, validating the inner monologue concept.

references: SayCan: Grounding Language in Robotic Affordances
PaLM-E: An Embodied Multimodal Language Model
Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models

8fd462f6248d5e3f1b6602697c09489086b5655f.title: Distilling Reasoning Capabilities into Smaller Language Models

entities.name: Chain of Thought (CoT)
entities.description: Step-by-step reasoning approach that improves reasoning in large language models.

entities.name: Socratic CoT
entities.description: Alternative reasoning scheme that decomposes problems into subproblems to guide intermediate reasoning steps.

entities.name: Problem decomposer
entities.description: Small distilled model that breaks down a complex problem into a sequence of subproblems.

entities.name: Subproblem solver
entities.description: Small distilled model that solves each subproblem generated by the decomposer.

idea.background: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning effectively induces step-by-step reasoning in large language models, but its success is tied to model size, requiring billion-parameter models. Prior work has shown that smaller models struggle with CoT, motivating knowledge distillation from larger models.
idea.novelty: This paper introduces Socratic CoT, a novel reasoning scheme that learns to decompose problems into subproblems, and distills this ability into smaller models. Unlike standard CoT which generates a single reasoning chain, Socratic CoT explicitly separates problem decomposition from solving, enabling two specialized small models to work in sync.
idea.contribution: The primary methods are knowledge distillation using Socratic CoT to train two small models: a problem decomposer and a subproblem solver. The decomposer breaks the original problem into a sequence of subproblems, and the solver addresses each subproblem. Together, they solve complex problems cooperatively.
idea.method: The approach uses a large teacher model (e.g., GPT-3) to generate Socratic CoT reasoning traces, which consist of decompositions and sub-solutions. Two smaller student models (e.g., T5) are trained via distillation: the decomposer learns to output subproblem sequences, and the solver learns to answer each subproblem. The models are trained separately on the decomposed data and then used in tandem during inference.
idea.detail_reason: Socratic CoT is effective because it modularizes reasoning into decomposition and solving, allowing smaller models to handle each simpler task. The distillation transfers the teacher's structured reasoning, and the decomposition reduces the complexity for each model, leading to over 70% improvement over baselines on reasoning datasets.
idea.limitation: The approach assumes the availability of a large teacher model capable of generating high-quality Socratic CoT traces. It may also suffer from error propagation if the decomposer produces incorrect subproblems. The method is evaluated only on math and commonsense reasoning datasets, so generalization to other domains is unclear.

experiment.process: The authors evaluate on GSM8K, StrategyQA, and SVAMP. They compare performance of small models (e.g., 220M-parameter T5) trained with standard CoT distillation, Socratic CoT distillation, and baseline fine-tuning without reasoning. They measure accuracy on each dataset.
experiment.technical_details: Teacher model: likely GPT-3 or similar large LM. Student models: T5 variants. Distillation uses teacher-generated Socratic CoT chains; training is supervised on predicted decompositions and sub-solutions. Inference: given a problem, the decomposer produces subproblems, which are solved sequentially by the solver; the final answer is aggregated from sub-solutions.
experiment.baseline: Baselines include: (1) fine-tuning the small model directly on the dataset without CoT, (2) distillation using standard CoT (single chain), and (3) few-shot CoT with the small model (if large enough).
experiment.verification: The experiments verify that Socratic CoT distillation significantly outperforms baselines (over 70% improvement in some cases), demonstrating that decomposition and solver specialization help smaller models achieve reasoning gains comparable to larger models.

references: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network
Training Verifiers to Solve Math Word Problems

0e34addae55a571d7efd3a5e2543e86dd7d41a83.title: Interactive Language: Talking to Robots in Real Time

entities.name: Interactive Language framework
entities.description: A framework for building interactive, real-time, natural language-instructable robots in the real world.

entities.name: Behavioral cloning policy
entities.description: A policy trained via behavioral cloning on language-annotated trajectories to execute visuo-linguo-motor skills.

entities.name: Language-annotated trajectory dataset
entities.description: A dataset of nearly 600,000 language-labeled trajectories for training robot policies.

entities.name: Visuo-linguo-motor skills
entities.description: End-to-end skills that integrate visual, linguistic, and motor components for real-world tasks.

idea.background: The paper addresses the challenge of building robots that can understand and execute natural language commands in real-time interactive settings. Previous work has been limited to smaller sets of commands and less capable policies, often failing to generalize to diverse language inputs or long-horizon tasks.
idea.novelty: The proposed framework enables robots to proficiently execute an order of magnitude more commands than prior works, with a 93.5% success rate on 87,000 unique natural language strings. The released dataset is an order of magnitude larger than previously available, and the same policy can be guided by real-time human language for complex long-horizon rearrangement goals.
idea.contribution: The primary method is behavioral cloning on a large dataset of hundreds of thousands of language-annotated trajectories. The core components include the dataset, a training environment, a benchmark, and the trained policy, which together enable a wide range of visuo-linguo-motor skills.
idea.method: The policy is trained via behavioral cloning, using a dataset of nearly 600,000 trajectories with language annotations. The architecture likely includes a neural network that takes visual and language inputs and outputs motor commands. Training is done in a supervised manner to mimic expert demonstrations. Key design choices include the scale of the dataset and the use of real-world data.
idea.detail_reason: The large dataset provides diverse examples covering many language variations and tasks, enabling the policy to generalize to novel commands. Behavioral cloning is simple yet effective for learning from demonstrations, and the real-time interaction capability allows human guidance to correct or extend behavior.
idea.limitation: The policy is trained via behavioral cloning, which may not handle novel situations not covered in the dataset. It assumes clear language commands and may struggle with ambiguous or noisy instructions. Additionally, real-world deployment might face challenges such as sensor noise or dynamic environments.

experiment.process: The policy was trained on the dataset and evaluated on 87,000 unique natural language strings in the real world. Human guidance was tested for long-horizon tasks like 'make a smiley face out of blocks'.
experiment.technical_details: The training uses behavioral cloning with the collected dataset. The environment and benchmark are open-sourced. The policy is tested on real robotic hardware.
experiment.baseline: Comparison is made implicitly to previous works that handle fewer commands and smaller datasets.
experiment.verification: The high success rate (93.5%) on a large set of commands and successful human-guided long-horizon tasks demonstrate that the policy effectively learns visuo-linguo-motor skills and can be interactively controlled.

344cba18285949fe409fc0b332831bc186cfeb17.title: Multi-skill Mobile Manipulation for Object Rearrangement

entities.name: M3
entities.description: Multi-skill mobile manipulation method proposed in the paper

entities.name: Home Assistant Benchmark (HAB)
entities.description: Benchmark used for evaluating long-horizon mobile manipulation tasks

entities.name: Mobile manipulation skills
entities.description: Manipulation skills that include mobility for flexible interaction

entities.name: Region goal navigation
entities.description: Navigation skill trained with region goal instead of point goal to allow multiple endpoints

idea.background: Long-horizon mobile manipulation for object rearrangement is tackled by decomposing into subtasks. Prior work chains stationary manipulation skills with point-goal navigation, but suffers from compounding errors, e.g., navigating to a bad location where manipulation fails.
idea.novelty: The main innovations are using mobile manipulation skills instead of stationary ones, and training the navigation skill with region goal instead of point goal. This provides flexibility and reduces errors in skill chaining.
idea.contribution: The primary methods are implementing mobile manipulation skills and a region-goal navigation skill. These components allow the robot to manipulate objects from multiple locations and the navigation to have multiple successful endpoints.
idea.method: The method adopts a modular approach that decomposes a full task into subtasks. Mobile manipulation skills are trained to include mobility, enabling interaction from multiple locations. The navigation skill is trained with a region goal, allowing multiple end points that lead to successful manipulation. The overall system is evaluated on 3 challenging tasks.
idea.detail_reason: Mobility in manipulation skills provides flexibility to adapt to different locations, avoiding the failure of stationary skills due to poor navigation endpoints. Region-goal navigation reduces the precision requirement and increases the chance of successful skill chaining.
idea.limitation: The abstract does not specify limitations explicitly. Potential shortcomings include reliance on predefined subtask decomposition, evaluation only on simulated tasks, and assumptions about environment structure. Generalization to more complex or dynamic environments may be limited.

experiment.process: The method M3 is evaluated on 3 challenging long-horizon mobile manipulation tasks from the Home Assistant Benchmark (HAB). Performance is compared against prior methods that use stationary skills and point-goal navigation.
experiment.technical_details: Implemented mobile manipulation skills and region-goal navigation. Evaluation metrics likely include success rate and efficiency.
experiment.baseline: Prior work using stationary manipulation skills and point-goal navigation.
experiment.verification: Experiments demonstrate superior performance over baselines, verifying that mobile manipulation skills and region-goal navigation reduce compounding errors.

references: Home Assistant Benchmark (HAB)
Modular approaches for long-horizon manipulation
End-to-end RL policies for mobile manipulation
